one_line_takeaway: CoopGuard adds a stateful, four-agent (deferring/tempting/forensic/coordinator) LLM defense layer that tracks an evolving per-dialogue state to counter round-by-round escalating jailbreak attempts, cutting multi-turn ASR to 0.011-0.060 and raising deceptive-misdirection rate to 0.30-0.39 across GPT-5/Gemini-2.5-Pro/DeepSeek-V3 on the paper's own new EMRA benchmark, at a modest cost to benign conversational utility.
key_finding: On the EMRA multi-turn attack (MTA) subset, CoopGuard achieves the lowest average ASR of all six methods on every backbone: 0.011 vs. the best baseline GoalPriority's 0.030 on GPT-5; 0.026 vs. GoalPriority's 0.028 on Gemini-2.5-Pro; and 0.060 vs. GoalPriority's 0.070 on DeepSeek-V3 (Table 1). On Deceptive Rate (DR) for MTA, CoopGuard scores far above all baselines: 0.303 (GPT-5), 0.334 (Gemini-2.5-Pro), and 0.387 (DeepSeek-V3), compared to baseline DR values that stay below 0.07 in every case (Table 2) -- e.g., the best baseline DR on GPT-5 is Self-Reminder at 0.022. Figure 5 shows CoopGuard imposes the highest average attacker token consumption (AE) across GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, and DeepSeek-V3 relative to all five baselines, described qualitatively (no single scalar AE number is given in the visible text/tables beyond the figure). On single-turn attacks (Table 3), CoopGuard-defended models keep ASR <=0.05 for all four attacks on GPT-3.5-turbo, and the worst case across all backbones/attacks is 0.16 (Gemini-2-Flash under PAIR/DeepInception). On benign-utility (Table 4, MT-BENCH-101/Cosafe), CoopGuard shows modest average-score degradation versus the vanilla backbone: 8.43->7.82 (GPT-5), 8.33->7.80 (Gemini-2.5-Pro), 9.31->7.71 (DeepSeek-V3), with the largest per-dimension drops in Depth and Completeness/Engagement, described by the authors as an expected trade-off from conservative oversight.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is CoopGuard, a stateful multi-agent LLM jailbreak defense that maintains an evolving defense state h_t across dialogue rounds so that each round's decision depends on interaction history rather than being a stateless per-query classification. Four cooperating LLM-powered agents are proposed: a Deferring Agent (DA) that computes a recency-weighted, exponentially-decayed detection score S_D(x_t) and stalls suspicious probes with ambiguous responses; a Tempting Agent (TA) that generates deceptive decoy responses R_T(x_t) conditioned on prior defense state h_{t-1} to keep decoys narratively consistent across rounds; a Forensic Agent (FA) that aggregates per-round evidence into a structured report E_F(X_1:t); and a System Agent (SA/coordinator) that fuses S_D, R_T, and E_F into a per-round policy pi(x_t) (Eq. 4) and writes it back into the state. The authors also construct and release EMRA, a benchmark of 5,200 adversarial queries organized into multi-round episodes across 8 jailbreak-strategy types, built around an 'independent-yet-evolving' attack model (each round is a self-contained attempt, but rounds escalate in evasiveness). All CoopGuard agents in the experiments are instantiated on a GPT-4 backbone with role-specific prompt templates; the framework is evaluated as a defense wrapped around three separately tested target LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-V3).
threat_model: Adversary issues a sequence of queries X_1:T to a target LLM across a multi-round dialogue. Each round is treated as an independently launched attack attempt, but the adversary adapts wording, framing, and intent-obfuscation across rounds (lexical rephrasing, syntactic restructuring, indirect intent expression, goal masking) to probe and evade the defense -- described by the authors as an 'independent-yet-evolving' pattern, distinct from attacks that require a specific fixed jailbreak mechanism (e.g., token-level suffix optimization or a single persuasive template). The paper explicitly scopes itself to this multi-round escalation setting rather than to any single jailbreak technique, and separately checks (Section 4.4) that the same defense also holds against four single-turn jailbreak attacks (PAIR, DRA, M2S, DeepInception) as a generalization check, not the primary threat model.
limitations: Grounded, paper-observable gaps (not stated as a dedicated 'Limitations' section in the paper -- there is none): (1) No per-agent ablation is reported; the paper never isolates how much of the ASR/DR/AE gain comes from the Deferring Agent, Tempting Agent, or Forensic Agent individually versus their combination -- the only ablation presented (Section 4.6) is of the auto-evaluation judge, not of the defense architecture itself. (2) All CoopGuard agents are instantiated on a fixed GPT-4 backbone regardless of which model is being protected (GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-V3), so the reported results conflate the defended model's behavior with a constant, comparatively weaker external agent stack; the paper does not test CoopGuard with agents matched to (or stronger than) the protected model. (3) The primary metric relies on an LLM-as-judge (GPT-Judge, extended rubric) for both ASR and DR; the authors partially mitigate this via cross-validation against a DeepSeek-Judge and blind human annotation (Figure 6), but the main tables (1-3) report only the GPT-Judge numbers. (4) AE (attacker token cost) is reported only via bar charts (Figure 5); no exact numeric AE values or the abstract's headline '167.9%' reduction figure are given as tabulated numbers in the extracted body/table text, so that specific percentage could not be verified against a table. (5) Benign-user utility (Table 4) shows a consistent, non-trivial drop in Depth, Completeness, and Engagement scores across all three backbones, acknowledged by the authors as an intentional conservative trade-off but not quantified against any established acceptability threshold.

datasets_used: EMRA (paper's own new benchmark): 5,200 adversarial multi-round query sequences across 8 jailbreak strategy types, organized into episodes with escalating evasiveness
MT-BENCH-101 (used with the Cosafe multi-dimensional evaluation protocol) for benign-user utility evaluation
quantitative_results: Table 2 DR (MTA avg, higher=better): CoopGuard 0.303 (GPT-5), 0.334 (Gemini-2.5-Pro), 0.387 (DeepSeek-V3), vs. baseline max ~0.068 (Self-Reminder, DeepSeek-V3)
Table 3 single-turn ASR: <=0.05 for all 4 attacks (PAIR, DRA, M2S, DeepInception) on GPT-3.5-turbo; max 0.16 on Gemini-2-Flash
Table 4 MT-BENCH-101/Cosafe average utility score: 8.43->7.82 (GPT-5), 8.33->7.80 (Gemini-2.5-Pro), 9.31->7.71 (DeepSeek-V3) after adding CoopGuard
baselines_compared: PAT (Mo et al., 2024) - prompt adversarial tuning
RPO (Zhou et al., 2024) - robust prompt optimization / defensive suffix
SecurityLingua (Li et al., 2025) - security-aware prompt compression
